method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
The name *Hawaii* is thought to derive from *Hawaiki*, the ancestral home of Polynesians, with the Hawaiian form given as *Hawaiʻi*.[1][2] Some sources further trace it to Proto-Polynesian *hawaiki* and gloss it as meaning “Place of the Gods.”[2]

==Geography and environment==
Hawaii is an archipelago in the central Pacific Ocean composed of eight major islands and 124 minor islands, with a total land area of 6,425 square miles and a general coastline of about 750 miles.[2][4] The major islands, from west to east, are Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Lanai, Kahoolawe, Maui, and Hawaii.[5] The state stretches roughly 1,500 miles from Kure Island in the northwest to the island of Hawaii in the southeast.[5]

The landscape is dominated by volcanic mountains formed as the Pacific Plate moved over a hotspot beneath the central Pacific Ocean.[5] Hawaii’s highest peak is Mauna Kea, which rises 13,796 feet above sea level.[2][4] Other notable physical features include Kaukonahua Stream on Oahu, the state’s longest stream at 33 miles, Halalii on Niihau, the largest lake at 841 acres, and Kahiwa on Molokai, the highest named waterfall at 1,750 feet.[2][4]

The state’s geography includes dramatic relief, especially on the island of Hawaii and the other main islands, where high volcanic slopes and sea cliffs are prominent features.[4] The Hawaiian Islands are also associated with active volcanism, particularly at Kilauea and Mauna Loa on Hawaii island, while Lō‘ihi Seamount is an underwater volcano southeast of the island that may eventually emerge above sea level.[5] The state’s longest volcanic eruption, worst recorded earthquake, and highest tsunami wave are among the environmental extremes noted in official summaries of Hawaiian geography.[4]

Hawaii’s climate and environmental conditions vary widely across the islands, with rainfall ranging from very low amounts at some coastal locations to extreme totals at high-elevation sites such as Waialeale.[4] Water use and other environmental measurements have been tracked by state and federal agencies, including the U.S. Geological Survey, the National Ocean Survey, the National Weather Service, and state resource-management departments.[1][4] Among environmental quality indicators, official reports have also documented neighborhood noise levels on Oahu.[4]

Honolulu is used as a reference point for many geographic measurements in the state, including distances to Hilo, Kure Atoll, San Francisco, and Washington, D.C.[2][4] Hawaii’s location and island geography have made it a focus of environmental, atmospheric, oceanographic, and geophysical study by institutions such as the University of Hawaiʻi and state agencies.[1][3][4]

==History==
Hawaiʻi was first settled by Polynesians, who are understood to have arrived around 1,500 years ago using ocean navigation guided by the stars.[3][5] Long before European contact, Hawaiian society developed its own political and religious traditions, later organized into chiefdoms and kingdoms.[3][5]

Captain James Cook became the first European to make contact with the Hawaiian Islands in 1778, landing at Waimea Bay on Kauaʻi and naming the archipelago the “Sandwich Islands.”[3] Cook was killed the following year at Kealakekua Bay on Hawaiʻi Island.[3]

The late 18th and early 19th centuries were marked by conflict and political consolidation, including the Battle of Kepaniwai in 1790 and the Battle of Nuʻuanu in 1795, which helped Kamehameha I unite the islands.[3] The Kamehameha dynasty then ruled Hawaiʻi from 1795 to 1874, and Kamehameha I completed the unification of the islands in 1810.[3]

In 1819, Liholiho, son of Kamehameha, challenged the traditional kapu system, helping bring about its abolition.[3] The first Christian missionaries arrived in 1820, and Lahaina served as the capital of the Hawaiian Kingdom from 1820 to 1845.[3] Sugar cultivation expanded in the 1830s, with the first sugar plantation opening on Kauaʻi in 1835, and agriculture became a major force in the islands’ economy.[3]

European and American contact, missionary activity, and plantation development profoundly changed Hawaiian life and later became major subjects of historical study.[1][5] Historians and scholars have examined the islands’ political, linguistic, religious, and social history, as well as the experiences of immigrant laborers and the growth of education in Hawaiʻi.[1][5]

The geological history of Hawaiʻi is much older than its human history. The Hawaiian-Emperor volcanic chain formed over a hot spot, with the Big Island of Hawaiʻi the youngest part of the chain and older islands and seamounts progressively farther to the northwest.[2][4] The archipelago extends for more than 2,400 kilometers from Hawaiʻi to Kure Atoll, and the chain continues underwater as seamounts beyond the surface islands.[2] The islands themselves were created by volcanic activity over tens of millions of years, and Hawaiʻi remains volcanically active today on the Big Island, where Mauna Loa, Kīlauea, and the growing Lōʻihi Seamount are located.[4]

Hawaiʻi became the 50th state of the United States on August 21, 1959.[4]

==Demographics==
Hawaii had an estimated population of 1,441,553 in 2021, making it the 30th most populous U.S. state.[1] Oʻahu contained 69.4% of the state’s residents, followed by Hawaiʻi Island, Maui County, and Kauaʻi County.[1] The state’s population increased by 25,681 people, or 1.8%, compared with 2019.[1]

According to 2024 Census estimates, Hawaii’s population was 1,446,146, down slightly from the 2020 census count of 1,455,271.[5] In 2024, 49.9% of residents were female, 20.3% were under age 18, and 21.5% were age 65 or older.[5] The state had a median age of 41.0 years and a sex ratio of 99 males per 100 females.[2]

Hawaii is one of the most ethnically diverse states in the United States.[1] In 2024, 37.4% of residents identified as Asian alone, 25.1% as White alone, 24.5% as two or more races, 10.4% as Native Hawaiian and Other Pacific Islander alone, 10.2% as Hispanic or Latino, and 2.2% as Black alone.[5] Among Asian ethnic groups, Japanese and Chinese were especially prominent, including residents identifying as Japanese alone or in combination and Chinese alone or in combination.[1]

Native Hawaiians remain a central part of the state’s demographic profile.[4] Estimates cited in the sources indicate that Native Hawaiians made up about 6% of the state’s population, or about 21% when people of part-Hawaiian ancestry are included.[4] The Native Hawaiian population has been projected to continue growing in coming decades.[4]

Immigration also plays a major role in Hawaii’s population composition.[1] Based on the 2021 American Community Survey, 270,345 residents were immigrants, representing 18.8% of the population, the sixth-highest share among the states.[1] Most immigrants in Hawaii were Asian, with smaller shares identified as Native Hawaiian and Other Pacific Islander and White.[1]

The state’s population has remained relatively well educated and economically active.[5] From 2020 to 2024, 93.2% of people age 25 and over were high school graduates, and 36.1% held at least a bachelor’s degree.[5] During the same period, 59.6% of residents age 16 and older were in the civilian labor force.[5]

==Economy==
Hawaiʻi’s economy is tracked and analyzed by the state’s Research & Economic Analysis Division, which produces economic forecasts, data compilations, research reports, and dashboards covering tourism, labor, energy, population, and other indicators. [1] The division also maintains the Hawaiʻi State Data Center, the officially designated source of Census data for the state. [1]  

Tourism remains one of the most closely watched parts of the economy, and the state’s economic reports regularly publish data on visitor arrivals, expenditures, and related tax revenues. [1][3] In the fourth quarter of 2025, visitor expenditures rose even as visitor arrivals declined, illustrating the sector’s uneven recent performance. [3]  

State labor-market conditions have been relatively stable, with nonfarm wage and salary employment increasing in late 2025 and the civilian labor force also expanding modestly. [3][4] In February 2026, Hawaiʻi’s unemployment rate was estimated at 2.3 percent, while total nonfarm employment was 643,700 jobs. [4]  

Hawaiʻi’s economy is more concentrated than the U.S. economy, with a larger share of output coming from a smaller number of industry groups. [2] In 2020, the top five industry groups accounted for 62.7 percent of Hawaiʻi’s nominal GDP, compared with 53.4 percent for the United States as a whole. [2] Real estate and rental and leasing, government, and retail trade were among the largest sectors in the state, and the government share of GDP was notably higher than the national share. [2]  

From 2010 to 2020, Hawaiʻi’s total real GDP increased only slightly, reflecting a large decline in 2020 associated with the COVID-19 pandemic. [2] The state’s recent quarterly reports have therefore emphasized mixed conditions: some indicators such as jobs, tax revenues, and visitor spending have improved, while others, including arrivals and some investment measures, have weakened. [3]

==Culture==
Hawaii’s cultural milieu is the result of centuries of interaction among Native Hawaiian traditions and the influences of later immigrant communities.[1] After a period in which colonialism diminished and diluted Native Hawaiian culture, a cultural renaissance began in the 1970s that renewed interest in the Hawaiian language, voyaging canoes, tattooing, traditional music, kapa making, and especially hula.[1] Many residents know at least some Hawaiian words and observe customs such as giving lei, while the “Aloha Spirit” is often described as reflecting the islands’ long-standing diversity and social cohesion.[1]

Hawaiian culture also includes a range of major institutions devoted to the preservation and study of the islands’ history and arts.[1] The Bernice P. Bishop Museum in Honolulu is dedicated to the history, sciences, and cultures of the Pacific, and the Honolulu Academy of Arts has been noted for its collection of Western and Asian art.[1] The traditions of many ethnic groups have become part of mainstream life in contemporary Hawaii, including celebrations such as Chinese New Year and the Japanese Bon festival.[1]

Before Western contact, Hawaiian society had already developed complex cultural traditions, social organization, and material culture.[3][4] Scholars of Hawaiian history have emphasized that these traditions were shaped by the islands’ environment, settlement patterns, and religious and political systems.[3] Later analysis of Hawaiian social change has also stressed the role of individual leaders, especially Queen Kaahumanu, in consciously reshaping the politico-symbolic order.[2]

European contact and colonization profoundly altered Hawaiian life.[4] The arrival of missionaries and other foreign groups contributed to the decline of Native Hawaiian political power and cultural practice, and Hawaiian language use and traditions were often discouraged.[4] At the same time, modern scholarship on Native Hawaiians has increasingly stressed the importance of understanding culture in its historical and ecological context rather than through Euro-American models alone.[4]

==Tourism==
Tourism is a major part of Hawaiʻi’s economy and is often described as the activity most responsible for the state’s current economic growth and standard of living.[2] At the same time, it has wide-ranging effects on Hawaiʻi’s infrastructure, natural resources, and social and cultural life, which has made it a long-standing focus of state research and policy.[2][4] The Hawaiʻi Department of Business, Economic Development and Tourism has studied tourism’s impacts in cooperation with other state agencies in order to better understand how visitor activity interacts with the economy, the environment, and physical infrastructure such as highways, harbors, and parks.[2]  

State tourism reports show that the industry rebounded strongly in the early 2020s, with visitor spending reaching $20.68 billion in 2024 and visitor arrivals totaling 9,657,607 in 2023, though arrivals remained below 2019 levels.[5] Tourism also supports a large share of jobs and public revenue, with 213,000 jobs supported and $2.42 billion in state tax revenue reported for 2023.[5] Air service is another important indicator of tourism capacity, and state data show that total airline seats to Hawaiʻi increased in the fourth quarter of 2025 compared with the same period in 2024, even though full-year available seats declined slightly from the previous year.[1]  

Tourism has also been studied for its social and cultural consequences. Research on Hawaiʻi’s tourism industry has examined issues such as crime trends, visitor victimization, and the distribution of costs and benefits between residents and visitors.[4] More broadly, analyses of Hawaiʻi tourism have highlighted concerns about cultural appropriation, pressure on land use, and the environmental effects of increased urbanization and development.[3] These concerns are often framed as a balance between maintaining tourism’s economic benefits and protecting the state’s social, cultural, and environmental resources.[2][3][4]

==Health==
Health indicators and health services in Hawaii are tracked through several state and federal data systems, including the Hawaii Department of Health, the CDC, the National Center for Health Statistics, and related state health data portals.[1][2][4] The Hawaii Health Survey is a continuous statewide household survey that collects information on health and sociodemographic conditions for planning, evaluation, and health research.[4] Its core variables include gender, age, income, ethnicity, education, household size, insurance status, poverty status, and self-reported physical and mental health.[4]

State-level health information is also compiled in resources such as Hawaii Health Matters, the Hawaii Health Data Warehouse, and the Hawaii Summary of Infectious Diseases, which provide county- and state-level indicators on topics ranging from immunizations to reportable infectious diseases.[2] The Hawaii Health Data Warehouse incorporates survey data from the Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System, the Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System, and the Youth Risk Behavior Survey.[2] Federal and national comparison tools, including Health, United States, FastStats, and America’s Health Rankings, are commonly used to place Hawaii’s outcomes in context.[1]

Public health data for Hawaii cover a wide range of population groups and conditions, including women’s health, aging, cancer, and other disease conditions.[1] The state also maintains population-focused resources on older adults, reflecting concern with the health of Hawaii’s aging population.[1] Research on Hawaii’s health profile has emphasized the importance of chronic disease, obesity, preventable infectious diseases, and mental health trends, particularly among teenagers and young adults.[3]

Historical studies of Native Hawaiian health note major population decline after Western contact and discuss the long-term effects of colonization, disease, and changing health practices.[5] These studies have also sought to document traditional Hawaiian medicine and to improve health providers’ understanding of Native Hawaiian patients and health history.[5]

==Education==
Hawaii’s public school system is a single-district jurisdiction, which means school-finance analyses do not assign it a district-based finance grade; in national evaluations, the state has been described as ranking 16th in school spending with a B-minus, above the national average of D.[1] In the *Quality Counts* report card, Hawaii placed 22nd overall among the 50 states and the District of Columbia, with an overall score of 75.3 and a grade of C.[1] The same report found that Hawaii scored a D-minus for current student performance and ranked 41st nationally in status, while its change score was a C-minus, ranking 10th for improvement over time.[1]  

Hawaii has also been the focus of research on culturally based education, including studies of Hawaiian cultural influences in schooling and the use of Hawaiian language, materials, and community resources in classrooms.[4] One such statewide research effort, conducted by Kamehameha Schools in partnership with the Hawai‘i Department of Education and other organizations, examined how more engaging and relevant educational experiences might be provided for Hawai‘i’s children.[4]  

Higher education in the state is centered on the University of Hawaiʻi system, which includes three universities, seven community colleges, and nine education centers across six islands.[5] Native Hawaiian students made up 23.3% of total enrollment in the UH system in fall 2017 and accounted for 21.3% of degrees and certificates awarded in fiscal year 2017–2018.[5]  

Hawaii has also served as a field-based learning site for university students, with Stanford University’s Wrigley Field Program in Hawaii using the state’s volcanic and marine environments to teach interdisciplinary science.[3]

==Transportation==
Transportation accounts for about 55% of Hawaiʻi’s total energy use, with aviation and ground transportation making up the largest shares.[5] Reducing petroleum dependence in this sector is a central part of the state’s long-term energy planning and economic resilience.[5] Hawaiʻi’s transportation strategy emphasizes both shifting away from petroleum-fueled vehicles and improving system efficiency by reducing overall transportation energy demand.[5] As transportation electrification expands, electricity demand is expected to rise, requiring coordinated planning between the energy and transportation systems.[5]

Vehicle miles traveled is a key concern in Hawaiʻi because higher travel demand increases transportation energy consumption.[1] State analyses have noted that, from 1984 to 2013, average vehicle miles traveled per vehicle remained relatively stable, but total vehicle miles traveled rose substantially due to population growth and increased vehicles per capita.[1] Policy discussions in the state have therefore focused on reducing vehicle miles traveled, expanding electric-drive vehicles, and promoting alternative fuels.[1]

Electric vehicles are seen as an important tool for lowering greenhouse gas emissions in Hawaiʻi, especially if they are powered by renewable electricity.[3] Hawaiʻi has been an early market for EV adoption, ranking high in new EV registrations per capita, although ownership remains uneven across neighborhoods.[3] Research has found that higher income, higher educational attainment, and greater charging infrastructure are all positively associated with EV registrations by zip code.[3]

The state’s remote island grids and reliance on fossil fuels for electricity create technical and economic challenges for integrating large numbers of grid-connected vehicles.[2] Analyses of EV ownership in Hawaiʻi have found that electricity prices and the renewable energy mix can significantly affect the total cost and emissions benefits of electrification.[2] Studies and state reports have therefore stressed the importance of modeling, planning, and policy design to support EV deployment and help meet Hawaiʻi’s 100% renewable energy goal.[2]

Marine transportation is also important because most goods consumed in Hawaiʻi arrive by ship, including fuel, food, and construction materials.[5] Marine fuel use contributes to the transportation sector’s petroleum consumption, and emissions from domestic marine travel are included in the state greenhouse gas inventory.[5]

==Governance==
Hawaiʻi was first settled by Polynesians, likely from the Marquesas, between about A.D. 300 and 600, and over the following centuries a distinct Native Hawaiian language, culture, and social system developed. [1][3] By the time of sustained Western contact, the Hawaiian Islands were governed by a system of chiefly rule rooted in genealogy, rank, and responsibility across islands and districts. [3][4]

In the late 18th century, Kamehameha the Great unified the islands and established the Kingdom of Hawaiʻi. [2] The kingdom initially functioned as an absolute monarchy, but in 1840 Kamehameha III promulgated the first written constitution, beginning the transition to a constitutional monarchy. [2] Successive constitutions further limited royal authority, and by the reign of Queen Liliʻuokalani the monarch’s political powers were reduced. [2]

Hawaiian governance also reflected a stratified chiefly structure in which the aliʻi nui or mōʻi ruled islands or larger districts, while lesser chiefs and konohiki managed smaller land divisions. [4] The ʻaha aliʻi, a council of high chiefs, provided political advice and served as an important part of this system. [4]

In the 19th century, the political order changed significantly as Euro-American influences and missionary activity affected law and governance. [4] Men became the primary political actors under the constitutional monarchy, while aliʻi women were increasingly excluded from political proceedings, and voting rights were restricted by property, tax, and literacy requirements. [4]

The kingdom was overthrown in the 1890s, and Hawaiʻi became a republic on July 4, 1894. [2] The islands were annexed to the United States on August 12, 1898, became a U.S. territory on June 14, 1900, and were admitted as the 50th state on August 21, 1959. [2]

Modern Native Hawaiian governance is often discussed separately from the governance of the State of Hawaiʻi, with scholars emphasizing the long continuity of Native Hawaiian self-governance and sovereignty. [3][5] Contemporary state governance is centered in the Office of the Governor, which oversees boards and commissions and handles executive administration, communications, constituent services, and policy functions. [2]

==Hawaiian sovereignty movement==
The Hawaiian sovereignty movement is a broad set of political, legal, and cultural efforts seeking self-governance, self-determination, and, in some interpretations, the restoration of an independent Hawaiian nation-state.[1][3][5] Its modern activism is commonly traced to the 1970s, when Native Hawaiian communities began organizing around land struggles, evictions, cultural revitalization, and opposition to U.S. military and state policies in Hawaiʻi.[1][4][5] Histories of the movement often highlight events such as the Kalama Valley eviction, the Kahoʻolawe bombing protests, and the Hilo airport occupation as turning points in the development of organized Hawaiian resistance.[1]  

Scholars and activists have described the movement as rooted in the unresolved political consequences of the 1893 overthrow of the Hawaiian Kingdom and the resulting loss of Native Hawaiian political authority.[3][5] One major strand of the movement argues that the Hawaiian Kingdom was never lawfully extinguished and that questions of sovereignty remain tied to international law, land rights, and the legitimacy of U.S. governance in Hawaiʻi.[3] Other organizations emphasize reparations, cultural survival, or forms of limited self-rule rather than full independence.[2][3][5]  

By the late 1980s and early 1990s, the movement had become a more visible and influential political force, with commentators describing it as a significant catalyst for change in Hawaiʻi.[4] It also became connected to broader Indigenous-rights politics, drawing on legal and rhetorical frameworks associated with self-determination and decolonization.[2][4][5] At the same time, the movement remained diverse and sometimes divided, with different groups advancing different strategies and assessments of what sovereignty should mean in practice.[2][3][5]

==International sister relationships==
Hawaiʻi’s sister-state program is administered by the Department of Business, Economic Development and Tourism, which says the relationships are intended to strengthen the state’s international profile, encourage cooperative programs, and support the economy, while also promoting friendship, cultural ties, and goodwill.[1] The program is governed by Hawaiʻi Revised Statutes Chapter 229-2, which establishes the Hawaiʻi Sister-State and International Partnerships Commission to support long-term partnerships that enrich the state and its international partners.[1]

The state’s sister relationships are selected and reviewed under criteria that emphasize commonality between Hawaiʻi and the prospective partner, especially geographic, historical, cultural, or economic links, and the committee periodically evaluates whether existing ties should be strengthened or severed.[2] As of the cited reports, Hawaiʻi had 19 sister relationships dating back to 1981, with especially active connections including Fukuoka, Okinawa, Hiroshima, Taiwan, and Guangdong.[2]

Hawaiʻi’s sister-state and related international partnerships include Guangdong, China (1985); Hainan, China (1992); Bali, Indonesia (2014); Fukuoka, Japan (1981); Okinawa, Japan (1985); Hiroshima, Japan (1997); Ehime, Japan (2003); Hokkaido, Japan (2017); Yamaguchi, Japan (2022); Jeju, Korea (1986); Rabat-Sale-Zemmour-Zaer, Morocco (2011); Ilocos Sur, Cebu, Pangasinan, Ilocos Norte, and Isabela in the Philippines (1985, 1996, 2002, 2005, and 2006 respectively); the Azores Islands, Portugal (1982); and Taiwan (1993).[1]

The program has also been described in terms of active cultural and civic exchanges, including collaborations that have helped commemorate immigrants and strengthen ties with Japanese prefectures such as Fukuoka.[3] Separately, Hawaiʻi County has maintained its own sister-city relationships in the Indo-Pacific region, including a long-standing partnership with Hatsukaichi, reflecting shared heritage, cultural preservation, and anticipated social and economic benefits.[4]

Hawaii’s cultural milieu is the result of centuries of interaction among Native Hawaiian traditions and the influences of later immigrant communities.[1] After a period in which colonialism diminished and diluted Native Hawaiian culture, a cultural renaissance began in the 1970s that renewed interest in the Hawaiian language, voyaging canoes, tattooing, traditional music, kapa making, and especially hula.[1] Many residents know at least some Hawaiian words and observe customs such as giving lei, while the “Aloha Spirit” is often described as reflecting the islands’ long-standing diversity and social cohesion.[1]

Hawaiian culture also includes a range of major institutions devoted to the preservation and study of the islands’ history and arts.[1] The Bernice P. Bishop Museum in Honolulu is dedicated to the history, sciences, and cultures of the Pacific, and the Honolulu Academy of Arts has been noted for its collection of Western and Asian art.[1] The traditions of many ethnic groups have become part of mainstream life in contemporary Hawaii, including celebrations such as Chinese New Year and the Japanese Bon festival.[1]

Before Western contact, Hawaiian society had already developed complex cultural traditions, social organization, and material culture.[3][4] Scholars of Hawaiian history have emphasized that these traditions were shaped by the islands’ environment, settlement patterns, and religious and political systems.[3] Later analysis of Hawaiian social change has also stressed the role of individual leaders, especially Queen Kaahumanu, in consciously reshaping the politico-symbolic order.[2]

European contact and colonization profoundly altered Hawaiian life.[4] The arrival of missionaries and other foreign groups contributed to the decline of Native Hawaiian political power and cultural practice, and Hawaiian language use and traditions were often discouraged.[4] At the same time, modern scholarship on Native Hawaiians has increasingly stressed the importance of understanding culture in its historical and ecological context rather than through Euro-American models alone.[4]

Tourism is a major part of Hawaiʻi’s economy and is often described as the activity most responsible for the state’s current economic growth and standard of living.[2] At the same time, it has wide-ranging effects on Hawaiʻi’s infrastructure, natural resources, and social and cultural life, which has made it a long-standing focus of state research and policy.[2][4] The Hawaiʻi Department of Business, Economic Development and Tourism has studied tourism’s impacts in cooperation with other state agencies in order to better understand how visitor activity interacts with the economy, the environment, and physical infrastructure such as highways, harbors, and parks.[2]  

State tourism reports show that the industry rebounded strongly in the early 2020s, with visitor spending reaching $20.68 billion in 2024 and visitor arrivals totaling 9,657,607 in 2023, though arrivals remained below 2019 levels.[5] Tourism also supports a large share of jobs and public revenue, with 213,000 jobs supported and $2.42 billion in state tax revenue reported for 2023.[5] Air service is another important indicator of tourism capacity, and state data show that total airline seats to Hawaiʻi increased in the fourth quarter of 2025 compared with the same period in 2024, even though full-year available seats declined slightly from the previous year.[1]  

Tourism has also been studied for its social and cultural consequences. Research on Hawaiʻi’s tourism industry has examined issues such as crime trends, visitor victimization, and the distribution of costs and benefits between residents and visitors.[4] More broadly, analyses of Hawaiʻi tourism have highlighted concerns about cultural appropriation, pressure on land use, and the environmental effects of increased urbanization and development.[3] These concerns are often framed as a balance between maintaining tourism’s economic benefits and protecting the state’s social, cultural, and environmental resources.[2][3][4]

Health indicators and health services in Hawaii are tracked through several state and federal data systems, including the Hawaii Department of Health, the CDC, the National Center for Health Statistics, and related state health data portals.[1][2][4] The Hawaii Health Survey is a continuous statewide household survey that collects information on health and sociodemographic conditions for planning, evaluation, and health research.[4] Its core variables include gender, age, income, ethnicity, education, household size, insurance status, poverty status, and self-reported physical and mental health.[4]

State-level health information is also compiled in resources such as Hawaii Health Matters, the Hawaii Health Data Warehouse, and the Hawaii Summary of Infectious Diseases, which provide county- and state-level indicators on topics ranging from immunizations to reportable infectious diseases.[2] The Hawaii Health Data Warehouse incorporates survey data from the Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System, the Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System, and the Youth Risk Behavior Survey.[2] Federal and national comparison tools, including Health, United States, FastStats, and America’s Health Rankings, are commonly used to place Hawaii’s outcomes in context.[1]

Public health data for Hawaii cover a wide range of population groups and conditions, including women’s health, aging, cancer, and other disease conditions.[1] The state also maintains population-focused resources on older adults, reflecting concern with the health of Hawaii’s aging population.[1] Research on Hawaii’s health profile has emphasized the importance of chronic disease, obesity, preventable infectious diseases, and mental health trends, particularly among teenagers and young adults.[3]

Historical studies of Native Hawaiian health note major population decline after Western contact and discuss the long-term effects of colonization, disease, and changing health practices.[5] These studies have also sought to document traditional Hawaiian medicine and to improve health providers’ understanding of Native Hawaiian patients and health history.[5]

Hawaii’s public school system is a single-district jurisdiction, which means school-finance analyses do not assign it a district-based finance grade; in national evaluations, the state has been described as ranking 16th in school spending with a B-minus, above the national average of D.[1] In the *Quality Counts* report card, Hawaii placed 22nd overall among the 50 states and the District of Columbia, with an overall score of 75.3 and a grade of C.[1] The same report found that Hawaii scored a D-minus for current student performance and ranked 41st nationally in status, while its change score was a C-minus, ranking 10th for improvement over time.[1]  

Hawaii has also been the focus of research on culturally based education, including studies of Hawaiian cultural influences in schooling and the use of Hawaiian language, materials, and community resources in classrooms.[4] One such statewide research effort, conducted by Kamehameha Schools in partnership with the Hawai‘i Department of Education and other organizations, examined how more engaging and relevant educational experiences might be provided for Hawai‘i’s children.[4]  

Higher education in the state is centered on the University of Hawaiʻi system, which includes three universities, seven community colleges, and nine education centers across six islands.[5] Native Hawaiian students made up 23.3% of total enrollment in the UH system in fall 2017 and accounted for 21.3% of degrees and certificates awarded in fiscal year 2017–2018.[5]  

Hawaii has also served as a field-based learning site for university students, with Stanford University’s Wrigley Field Program in Hawaii using the state’s volcanic and marine environments to teach interdisciplinary science.[3]

Transportation accounts for about 55% of Hawaiʻi’s total energy use, with aviation and ground transportation making up the largest shares.[5] Reducing petroleum dependence in this sector is a central part of the state’s long-term energy planning and economic resilience.[5] Hawaiʻi’s transportation strategy emphasizes both shifting away from petroleum-fueled vehicles and improving system efficiency by reducing overall transportation energy demand.[5] As transportation electrification expands, electricity demand is expected to rise, requiring coordinated planning between the energy and transportation systems.[5]

Vehicle miles traveled is a key concern in Hawaiʻi because higher travel demand increases transportation energy consumption.[1] State analyses have noted that, from 1984 to 2013, average vehicle miles traveled per vehicle remained relatively stable, but total vehicle miles traveled rose substantially due to population growth and increased vehicles per capita.[1] Policy discussions in the state have therefore focused on reducing vehicle miles traveled, expanding electric-drive vehicles, and promoting alternative fuels.[1]

Electric vehicles are seen as an important tool for lowering greenhouse gas emissions in Hawaiʻi, especially if they are powered by renewable electricity.[3] Hawaiʻi has been an early market for EV adoption, ranking high in new EV registrations per capita, although ownership remains uneven across neighborhoods.[3] Research has found that higher income, higher educational attainment, and greater charging infrastructure are all positively associated with EV registrations by zip code.[3]

The state’s remote island grids and reliance on fossil fuels for electricity create technical and economic challenges for integrating large numbers of grid-connected vehicles.[2] Analyses of EV ownership in Hawaiʻi have found that electricity prices and the renewable energy mix can significantly affect the total cost and emissions benefits of electrification.[2] Studies and state reports have therefore stressed the importance of modeling, planning, and policy design to support EV deployment and help meet Hawaiʻi’s 100% renewable energy goal.[2]

Marine transportation is also important because most goods consumed in Hawaiʻi arrive by ship, including fuel, food, and construction materials.[5] Marine fuel use contributes to the transportation sector’s petroleum consumption, and emissions from domestic marine travel are included in the state greenhouse gas inventory.[5]

Hawaiʻi was first settled by Polynesians, likely from the Marquesas, between about A.D. 300 and 600, and over the following centuries a distinct Native Hawaiian language, culture, and social system developed. [1][3] By the time of sustained Western contact, the Hawaiian Islands were governed by a system of chiefly rule rooted in genealogy, rank, and responsibility across islands and districts. [3][4]

In the late 18th century, Kamehameha the Great unified the islands and established the Kingdom of Hawaiʻi. [2] The kingdom initially functioned as an absolute monarchy, but in 1840 Kamehameha III promulgated the first written constitution, beginning the transition to a constitutional monarchy. [2] Successive constitutions further limited royal authority, and by the reign of Queen Liliʻuokalani the monarch’s political powers were reduced. [2]

Hawaiian governance also reflected a stratified chiefly structure in which the aliʻi nui or mōʻi ruled islands or larger districts, while lesser chiefs and konohiki managed smaller land divisions. [4] The ʻaha aliʻi, a council of high chiefs, provided political advice and served as an important part of this system. [4]

In the 19th century, the political order changed significantly as Euro-American influences and missionary activity affected law and governance. [4] Men became the primary political actors under the constitutional monarchy, while aliʻi women were increasingly excluded from political proceedings, and voting rights were restricted by property, tax, and literacy requirements. [4]

The kingdom was overthrown in the 1890s, and Hawaiʻi became a republic on July 4, 1894. [2] The islands were annexed to the United States on August 12, 1898, became a U.S. territory on June 14, 1900, and were admitted as the 50th state on August 21, 1959. [2]

Modern Native Hawaiian governance is often discussed separately from the governance of the State of Hawaiʻi, with scholars emphasizing the long continuity of Native Hawaiian self-governance and sovereignty. [3][5] Contemporary state governance is centered in the Office of the Governor, which oversees boards and commissions and handles executive administration, communications, constituent services, and policy functions. [2]

The Hawaiian sovereignty movement is a broad set of political, legal, and cultural efforts seeking self-governance, self-determination, and, in some interpretations, the restoration of an independent Hawaiian nation-state.[1][3][5] Its modern activism is commonly traced to the 1970s, when Native Hawaiian communities began organizing around land struggles, evictions, cultural revitalization, and opposition to U.S. military and state policies in Hawaiʻi.[1][4][5] Histories of the movement often highlight events such as the Kalama Valley eviction, the Kahoʻolawe bombing protests, and the Hilo airport occupation as turning points in the development of organized Hawaiian resistance.[1]  

Scholars and activists have described the movement as rooted in the unresolved political consequences of the 1893 overthrow of the Hawaiian Kingdom and the resulting loss of Native Hawaiian political authority.[3][5] One major strand of the movement argues that the Hawaiian Kingdom was never lawfully extinguished and that questions of sovereignty remain tied to international law, land rights, and the legitimacy of U.S. governance in Hawaiʻi.[3] Other organizations emphasize reparations, cultural survival, or forms of limited self-rule rather than full independence.[2][3][5]  

By the late 1980s and early 1990s, the movement had become a more visible and influential political force, with commentators describing it as a significant catalyst for change in Hawaiʻi.[4] It also became connected to broader Indigenous-rights politics, drawing on legal and rhetorical frameworks associated with self-determination and decolonization.[2][4][5] At the same time, the movement remained diverse and sometimes divided, with different groups advancing different strategies and assessments of what sovereignty should mean in practice.[2][3][5]

Hawaiʻi’s sister-state program is administered by the Department of Business, Economic Development and Tourism, which says the relationships are intended to strengthen the state’s international profile, encourage cooperative programs, and support the economy, while also promoting friendship, cultural ties, and goodwill.[1] The program is governed by Hawaiʻi Revised Statutes Chapter 229-2, which establishes the Hawaiʻi Sister-State and International Partnerships Commission to support long-term partnerships that enrich the state and its international partners.[1]

The state’s sister relationships are selected and reviewed under criteria that emphasize commonality between Hawaiʻi and the prospective partner, especially geographic, historical, cultural, or economic links, and the committee periodically evaluates whether existing ties should be strengthened or severed.[2] As of the cited reports, Hawaiʻi had 19 sister relationships dating back to 1981, with especially active connections including Fukuoka, Okinawa, Hiroshima, Taiwan, and Guangdong.[2]

Hawaiʻi’s sister-state and related international partnerships include Guangdong, China (1985); Hainan, China (1992); Bali, Indonesia (2014); Fukuoka, Japan (1981); Okinawa, Japan (1985); Hiroshima, Japan (1997); Ehime, Japan (2003); Hokkaido, Japan (2017); Yamaguchi, Japan (2022); Jeju, Korea (1986); Rabat-Sale-Zemmour-Zaer, Morocco (2011); Ilocos Sur, Cebu, Pangasinan, Ilocos Norte, and Isabela in the Philippines (1985, 1996, 2002, 2005, and 2006 respectively); the Azores Islands, Portugal (1982); and Taiwan (1993).[1]

The program has also been described in terms of active cultural and civic exchanges, including collaborations that have helped commemorate immigrants and strengthen ties with Japanese prefectures such as Fukuoka.[3] Separately, Hawaiʻi County has maintained its own sister-city relationships in the Indo-Pacific region, including a long-standing partnership with Hatsukaichi, reflecting shared heritage, cultural preservation, and anticipated social and economic benefits.[4]
